posts.caption:
The Cannes Film Festival, the world’s largest cinematic showcase, has become a litmus test for the industry’s evolving relationship with AI.  The 79th edition in France saw the event enter a multi-year partnership with Meta AI, replacing TikTok. It is a move consistent with tech entrepreneur Mark Zuckerberg’s attempt to move into soft power, and comes right after he was spotted at the Met Gala sponsored by fellow tech billionaire Jeff Bezos and wife Lauren Sanchez. Meta’s role is to ensure frenzied coverage of the festival, and respond to filmmakers’ openness to using AI tools. With the Ray-Ban meta glasses content creators are capturing photos and videos and livestreaming in real time. The aim—to make the festival accessible to audiences across the world. Cannes’ embrace of Meta feels jarring, especially in the face of The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences’ announcement that only acting and writing for films done by humans will be considered eligible to win an Oscar. This declaration came after Dutch actor-producer Eline Van der Velden unveiled Tilly Norwood, the “world’s first AI actress” at Zurich Film Festival in September last year.
At Cannes, there is a sense of resignation and curiosity. Steven Soderbergh’s documentary on John Lennon and Yoko Ono, called John Lennon: The Last Interview, has used Meta’s AI tools to generate surreal graphics. Directors like Xavier Gens and Peter Jackson have expressed no qualms about working with the technology, but in the same breath emphasised the need to protect creatives. It is a dull, diplomatic tirade that skirts around the seismic shift this is likely to bring to filmmaking. Actor and jury member Demi Moore stated that the battle against AI is one humans are likely to lose—one of the more honest, albeit cynical, responses among the cohort. Director James Gray, whose Paper Tiger has emerged as a festival favourite, had something humanistic to offer: “I don’t think in our lifetime, or even our children’s lifetimes, it will come close to mirroring the only true infinite we know, which is the soul.” Whether Cannes continues to be soulmates with AI or not, only time (and Meta) will tell.
🖋️ @shrutijn6891

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
Spinster was a legal term in England until 2005 😟
Not ancient history or a relic of the far off, distant past.
Women currently alive were referred to in official legal documents by a medieval textile job title that had been weaponised into an insult.
Because that’s what a spinster was originally. A job title for a woman who spins wool into yarn. A job that involved skilled labour and was essential to the entire pre-industrial economy.
Then unmarried women got pushed out of better-paid textile work (because they didn’t have husbands with trade connections) and into spinning. The lowest-status work that was left for them.
So the job title became a proxy for marital status and by the 17th century it was the legal term for an unmarried woman in official documents.
Full episode on The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Ep 3: The Dictionary Was Named After a Woman Weaver. Link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on May 18, 2026. May be an image of text that says ''The Goddess TheGoddessProject Project Spinster mas a job title. Then it became an insult. veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
My married surname is Tucker. I’ve been using it for years without knowing what it meant.
Tucker comes from the Old English tucian which means to torment or beat cloth. A tucker was a cloth finisher: someone who took raw woven fabric off the loom and scoured it, beat it, trampled it in water until it became finished cloth ready to wear.
It was skilled physical labour that was essential to the entire textile trade.
The same job had three names depending on where you were in England. Fuller in the north. Walker in Scotland and the Midlands. Tucker in the West Country…Devon, Somerset, Wiltshire, Dorset, Cornwall.
My husband’s patrilineal family came from Plymouth and Cornwall.
The name came from exactly the place where it originated, where the cloth trade shaped the landscape and the language for centuries.
And Cornwall isn’t simply the far end of England, Kernow is one of the six Celtic nations with a textile tradition that predates the Norman conquest by centuries.
I am a textile and fashion designer and I married into the name of a cloth worker from a Celtic textile region without knowing any of this.
This beautiful man and his name found me 🫶🏼😭
Tucker is one surname and there are dozens more carrying the same secret and I’m going deep on more of them in the next episode of my podcast, The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Link in bio.

posts.caption:
“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”
For the May Issue of Vogue Adria, we spoke with @SlavojZizeks, one of today’s most renowned philosophers, about the future of the left, the dangers facing modern society, one night stands, and revolution.

posts.caption:
The women’s world number one @ArynaSabalenka understands that tennis can be opera: full of triumph and despair, grace and slapstick, sin and atonement. The 28-year-old has been at the top of her sport for the last year and a half, with a deep bench of brand associations—including with Gucci, Nike, and Audemars Piguet—to show for it. But since her earliest days on the tour, she’s been a fiery on-court presence, known for her outbursts (she’s a world-class racket-smasher) and tense exchanges with officials. (She attributes much of this to being “very much a Taurus”).
Time and training have helped her to temper that fire—but it also has its uses. “This is part of my personality that can drive me crazy, but it can also drive me into that real fight mode and help me play with passion,” Sabalenka says. “It’s two sides of the medal.”
For Vogue’s May 2026 digital cover, @robertjhaskell meets Aryna Sabalenka in Rome to talk winning, losing, grieving, learning, and never letting up.
Photographed by @stevenkleinstudio,Written by @robertjhaskell, Styled by @tonnegood, Hair by @sandyhullett, Makeup by @markcarrasquillo, Manicure by @nailsbyetsuko, Tailoring by @sara.on.set.tailor, Produced by @thatoneproduction, Shot on Location at @faenamiamibeach. posts.caption: Nicolas Ghesquière’s 2027 resort show for Louis Vuitton had two chapters. The first was dedicated to Pop Art, via a collaboration with Keith Haring; the second to the pomp of the Gilded Age. Vogue was launched during that time of growth and industrialization (the 1870-1890s), so we looked back through the magazine’s earliest issues to document the fashions of that distant past. Tap the link in bio to see the entire LV resort collection.

posts.caption: What do Pop and travel or uptown and downtown have in common? They all came together in @NicolasGhesquière’s imagination as he dreamed up his latest @louisvuitton collection. While suitcases have long been LV's whole thing, the one that inspired Ghesquière’s 2027 resort collection for the house is something else altogether. Learn more at the link in bio.

posts.caption: This season marked Sydney Fashion Week’s 30th anniversary, and the Resort 2027 collections have settled into a new routine, finding a new home and a refreshed look. Many of this season’s shows were anchored in and around the city’s Museum of Contemporary Art, which boasts a newly minted runway space on the ground floor. The most unifying trend on the runways actually came from a noticeable pattern in the casting: almost every brand, both established and emerging, featured a refreshing dash of mature models. Australia’s own muse, model Rachel Waller, was a season regular, walking in six shows. Check out the next generation of Australia’s independent designers at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Barcelona-based tastemaker @BlancaMiro Scrimieri, has built a name—and a fashion label—around her love for joyful color and bold creative expression. “I don’t separate fashion, interiors, or beauty—it’s all the same conversation for me,” the co-founder of La Veste shares. Whether it’s playing with texture or piling on prints, she adds, “I love mixing things that maybe shouldn’t work together but surprisingly do, pairing something quite proper next to something a little off.” Shop her wishlist at the link in our bio.

@maudeapatow is ready now. Fresh off her return in the highly anticipated third season of #Euphoria, Apatow is stepping into a new chapter with the kind of clarity and control that only comes from growing up in real time—on-screen, off-screen, and alongside co-stars who have gone on to navigate intergalactic levels of fame and careers of their own. "Just thinking about us being little kids and having no idea what was in store to now… It's insane," she says.
In our May cover story, Apatow opens up about the roles that shaped her, the relationships that fulfilled her, the theater work that grounded her, and the rituals—travel, matcha, and all—that keep her moving forward. Read our full interview with the actress and director at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@lilamoss is having the time of her life. At just 23, Moss has already graced the cover of British Vogue, starred in a @marcjacobs beauty campaign, and walked for @coperni, @versace, @miumiu, and @victoriassecret. But her path into fashion wasn’t always a given, despite having a supermodel mother. “I was always really interested in fashion,” she says. “I was always putting on my mom’s shoes and doing fake runways. But it was never something really that my mom wanted me to do.”
From her very first modeling opportunity, Moss knew she had found her lane. Now, she’s using her platform to advocate for causes close to her heart—from proudly wearing her type 1 diabetes tech in editorials to collaborating on the first-ever type 1 diabetic Barbie. See how Moss is carving her own path in our summer cover story at the link in our bio.

Before there was modern American interiors as we know it, there was Sister Parish. ⁠
⁠
The legendary decorator — known for shaping the private quarters of the Kennedy White House and pioneering an unmistakably layered, lived-in design language — became the ultimate arbiter of American design. ⁠
⁠
After founding her firm in 1933, Parish later built a design empire with Albert Hadley, counting the Astors, Rockefellers, Vanderbilts and Whitneys among her influential clientele. Now, her descendants are bringing that legacy into a new era.⁠
⁠
Read how in WWD Weekend's May issue at the link in bio.⁠
⁠
Report: @fiaceleste

posts.caption:
A few hours after “Ragtime” claimed five @dramaleague awards last Friday, @benlross was back in his dressing room at the Lincoln Center Theater, and getting ready to continue doing the work that has established the musical as an awards season frontrunner. ⁠
⁠
“I just keep saying to myself, ‘Even though you’re tired, take everything in, take every second in, keep your eyes open, look around,’” says Ross. “Because this is rare, and this does not happen all the time.’”⁠
⁠
In fact, it’s the 28-year-old actor’s first time being nominated (and eligible) for a Tony award. In recent weeks, he won Drama Desk and Outer Critics Circle awards for his featured performance in the musical.⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
